# 2. Data

## 2.1 Data sources and construction

We use four waves of the Ghana Living Standards Survey (GLSS): 1998/99 (GLSS4), 2005/06 (GLSS5), 2012/13 (GLSS6), and 2016/17 (GLSS7). These are repeated cross-sectional surveys that are representative at the national and regional levels. The GLSS uses a two-stage stratified sampling design. In the first stage, enumeration areas are selected as primary sampling units across Ghana's erstwhile ten regions. In the second stage, households are randomly selected within each enumeration area for detailed interviews.

Except for the resource extraction variables, all household- and farm-level variables come from a consolidated farmer-level GLSS dataset [@tsiboe2020] which has been used in several previous studies [@tsiboe2019; @tsiboe2021; @tsiboe2021a; @tsiboe2021b; @tsiboe2022; @gaku2023; @gaku2021]. We restrict the sample to crop-producing households and exclude crop yields below the 2.5th percentile or above the 97.5th percentile within each crop and survey wave to reduce the influence of extreme observations. The final sample contains 26,811 farm operators cultivating one or more of the following crops: banana, beans, cashew, cassava, citrus, cocoa, coconut, cocoyam, cotton, kenaf, maize, millet, palm, peanuts, plantain, potato, rice, sorghum, yam, sugarcane, tomato, pepper, okra, onion, eggplant, and other crops. All monetary values are deflated to real 2016/17 Ghana cedis (GH₵) using the national consumer price index, with the GLSS7 survey period as the base. Unless otherwise noted, all descriptive statistics and aggregate efficiency scores are computed using the GLSS household sampling weights, so reported means are representative of the surveyed population of crop producers.

The consolidated dataset does not contain direct community-level measures of extractive activity. We therefore recover these indicators from the original GLSS community questionnaires and merge them into the farmer-level data using enumeration area identifiers. The community questionnaire records information on demographic characteristics, local economic activities, infrastructure, health and education facilities, agricultural practices, and development trends. In each community, interviewers collect this information from up to 20 opinion leaders, including chiefs, elders, assembly members, unit committee members, teachers, nurses, and other knowledgeable residents. The survey protocol requires these respondents to reflect the occupational, ethnic, gender, and religious diversity of the community.

We classify a community as an extraction locality if any of the four highest-ranked economic activities reported by community[^2] opinion leaders is extractive. Specifically, an enumeration area is classified as: (i) a mineral mining community if commercial or informal small-scale (galamsey) mining of gold, diamond, or bauxite is listed; (ii) a quarrying community if stone quarrying is listed; and (iii) a sand winning community if sand winning is listed. Because the questionnaire response options differ across rounds, the indicators are harmonized as follows. GLSS4 and GLSS5 record only undifferentiated "mining", so the distinction between commercial and informal or small-scale mining is observed only in GLSS6 and GLSS7, and the disaggregated estimates for these two categories therefore rest on the 2012/13 and 2016/17 rounds. For GLSS7, we additionally count communities where an extractive activity is reported as a major type of work performed by people who come to the community temporarily for work. Salt mining, which appears as a response option in all rounds, enters the aggregate extraction indicator but is too rare to support separate analysis (Table 2). Table A1 in the appendix details, for each survey round, the source variables, question wording, and the response options mapped to each extraction category.

Communities may report more than one extractive activity, so the aggregate indicator equals one if any is present, and the disaggregated indicators allow us to examine whether different extractive land uses are associated with different technology and efficiency outcomes. Under this definition, 11 percent of farmers in the sample reside in extraction communities. Extraction is, however, rarely the dominant local activity: counting only the top-ranked activity classifies well below 1 percent of farmers as exposed, rising to about 5 percent with the two highest-ranked activities and 11 percent with all four. The indicator therefore captures communities where extraction operates alongside, rather than instead of, agriculture, which is precisely the land-use competition setting this paper studies. Throughout the paper, "extraction community" refers to this community-level classification; all sampled farmers residing in a classified enumeration area are treated as exposed, regardless of their own occupation.

[^2]: The listed activities in the community questionnaire to be ranked included farming, fishing, trading, handicraft, salt mining, gold mining (commercial), gold mining (galamsey), diamond, bauxite, sand winning, and quarrying.

## 2.2 Descriptive statistics

Table 1 summarizes the main characteristics of crop producers in the pooled sample and by extraction status. The average farmer is about 47 years old, has four years of education, and lives in a household of about five adult-equivalent members. About one-quarter of producers are female, and the average dependency ratio is 1.42. The average farmer cultivates 1.84 hectares, and about 59 percent own their farmland. Mean crop output value is about GH₵ 1,049 per hectare in real terms, although there is substantial variation across crops and communities.

Input use remains low for some productivity-enhancing technologies. Average expenditure on planting materials is about GH₵ 100 per hectare, while farmers use about 20 hired labour man-days, 175 kg of fertilizer, and 13 litres of pesticide per hectare. Access to irrigation, mechanization, and credit is limited, with mean access rates of about 1%, 9%, and 17%, respectively. These descriptive patterns tell of a smallholder production environment in which productivity depends strongly on land, labour, and access to modern inputs.

The disaggregated summary statistics in Table A2 show that producers in extraction communities differ from the pooled sample in several respects, although the pattern varies by extraction activity. Farmers in mining communities tend to be older and more educated than the pooled average, while female representation is broadly similar to the pooled average across extraction categories, and slightly higher in sand-winning communities. Commercial mining communities record relatively high average yields for several crops, but the number of observations is small. Informal or small-scale mining communities show higher average fertilizer and pesticide use than the pooled sample, while quarrying communities show lower land size and lower planting material, fertilizer, and pesticide use. Sand winning communities have relatively high land ownership and credit access, but lower average planting material use. These differences reinforce the need to avoid treating all forms of extraction as equivalent.

Table A3 in the appendix reports trends in farmer, household, production, and input characteristics across the survey waves. In the pooled sample, education, household labour, irrigation access, and several crop yields increase over time, while average land size, crop diversification, mechanization, and credit access decline. Trends also differ by extraction activity. For instance, informal or small-scale mining communities record rising education levels and increases in several crop yields, but declining crop diversification and mechanization. Quarrying communities show increases in household labour and some input-use measures, while sand winning communities show declining mechanization and credit access. These patterns suggest that observed differences between extraction and non-extraction communities may reflect both extraction exposure and broader structural changes in Ghanaian agriculture.

Overall, the descriptive statistics provide two motivations for the empirical strategy. First, extraction communities differ from non-extraction communities in observable farmer, household, input, and crop characteristics, making simple mean comparisons insufficient. Second, the differences are not uniform across mining, quarrying, and sand winning. We therefore use matching to improve comparability across communities and a meta-stochastic frontier framework to distinguish technology gaps from differences in technical efficiency.
